episode0.title: THE DEAD ENGINEER — LIFELINE CUT V5

event3.text: I know I said we could avoid it.

event5.text: I am starting to think that was optimism wearing a lab coat.

event7.text: I'm still in the relay room. The floor is vibrating under my boots, and the carrier panel keeps throwing a thin red line across the wall.

event9.text: It looks like a warning. Or a pulse. I cannot decide which answer I hate more.

event11.text: Personnel cache is below this deck. If my file is still there, it should tell us whether the death record was real or corrupted.

event13.text: I am not ready to read it.

event16.text: That is not the same as refusing.

event1.text: You went straight for the thing I am trying not to look at.

event3.text: I understand why. I would have asked too.

event5.text: 7.14 was a backup emergency carrier. Low traffic. Ugly signal. Supposed to stay quiet unless the main channels died.

event10.text: I remember it answering before anyone transmitted.

event12.text: That is the part I cannot make technical enough to feel safe.

event14.text: I am leaving the relay room. I need both hands on the ladder, so I am cutting the channel until I reach the lower deck.

event3.text: I hate that the unknown signal gave useful advice. Useful horror is still horror.

event5.text: I'm moving now. Service ladder is open below the relay deck. No lift, no lights, just handrails and a lot of faith in old bolts.

event7.text: If I go quiet, it is because I need both hands. Not because something got me.

event3.text: Door is jammed halfway open. Enough for my shoulders if I turn sideways. Not enough for comfort.

event5.text: Manual crank on the wall. Maintenance panel under the frame. Both have scratches around them.

event7.text: The crank would move the door now. The panel might tell me who moved it before me.

event3.text: I have both boots braced and one hand on the crank. If this jams harder, I will deny making any sound.
I am kneeling under the frame. The floor is gritty. Sealant flakes, not dust.

event5.text: Give me a minute. I need to open this without waking the door log.

event0.title: PERSONNEL FILE: ETHAN HALE
event0.content:
DR. ETHAN HALE
ROLE: CHIEF ENGINEER
ASSIGNMENT: ORBITAL RESEARCH STATION ECHO
STATUS: DECEASED
TIME OF DEATH: 21:20
LOCATION: DOOR 12 ACCESS CORRIDOR
event0.caption: Recovered personnel record from corrupted station cache.
event0.message: [PERSONNEL FILE: ETHAN HALE ARCHIVED TO DATA BANK]

event3.text: Operator?

event8.text: I am reading it again because apparently I believe text might change if I stare hard enough.

event13.text: Time of death: 21:20. Door 12 access corridor.

event15.text: I can feel the deck under my boots. I can hear the relay in my ear.

event17.text: So either the file is wrong...

event19.text: or I am having a very difficult conversation from somewhere I do not understand.

event22.text: Do I sound alive to you?

event3.text: I am looking at the words and trying not to let them become the only thing in the room.

event5.text: My hands are cold. That could be the archive temperature. It could be panic. I am not running a diagnostic on my feelings right now.

event7.text: Medical archive should confirm or contradict the file.

event12.text: then I need a new category for myself.

event3.text: That is not scientific. I know that.

event5.text: But I can hear you. I can feel the floor shaking. I am scared enough to be very, very alive.

event7.text: I am going to hold on to that until evidence tears it out of my hands.

event9.text: Medical archive is the next place to check.

event1.text: Medical archive is past the observation corridor.

event3.text: I can see the corridor entrance from here. Long glass strip on the left. Dead lights along the floor.

event5.text: There is another way through the lab access hall. Slower. More turns. Less open glass.

event7.text: I do not know which one is better.

event9.text: I only know I do not want to stand here with my own death file open.

event3.text: I am clipping the radio to my suit. If I talk while crossing, every sound will feel like an answer.
Slower route. More doors. Fewer long sightlines. I am choosing to call that good.

event5.text: Going quiet while I move. The floor here is cluttered and I would like to keep both ankles.

event3.text: The screen woke up when I touched it. I almost apologized to it. That is where I am emotionally.

event4.title: AUTOPSY FRAGMENT: HALE
event4.content:
SUBJECT: ETHAN HALE
CAUSE OF DEATH: DECOMPRESSION TRAUMA
LOCATION: DOOR 12 ACCESS CORRIDOR
RECOVERY STATUS: BODY NOT RECOVERED
NOTE: VITALS TERMINATED AT 21:20
ARCHIVE INTEGRITY: 68%
event4.caption: Recovered emergency medical fragment.
event4.message: [AUTOPSY FRAGMENT: HALE ARCHIVED TO DATA BANK]

event10.text: That should feel better than recovered.

event14.text: If they found a body, at least the nightmare has a shape.

event16.text: Not recovered means the station either lost me...

event18.text: or found something it refused to call a body.

event20.text: Listen to me. I sound like a man arguing with a filing cabinet.

event22.text: Medical logs make tragedy look organized. I hate them for that.

event3.text: No. That is not honest.

event5.text: I know pieces. I just do not know if they are mine.

event7.text: I am moving to the relay analysis bay while I talk. Maybe walking will keep the memory from turning into a wall.

event1.text: I am connecting the live carrier to the analysis bay.

event3.text: The bay is cold enough that the console glass fogs when I breathe near it.

event0.title: TIMELINE CONFLICT
event0.content:
21:14 — Door 12 opened.
21:17 — Security override accepted.
21:20 — Ethan Hale declared deceased.
21:22 — Live communication signature still matches Ethan Hale.

Conflict: active communication signature exists after recorded time of death.
Secondary conflict: communication signature contains 7.14 MHz harmonic residue.
event0.caption: Automated timeline conflict report.

event4.text: The cleanest impossible thing we have found so far.

event6.text: The system says I died at 21:20. The relay says I kept talking after.

event8.text: And somewhere between those two statements, I am supposed to exist.

event11.text: We do not need another puzzle. We need to decide which part of reality gets trusted first.

choice0.text: TREAT THE DEATH RECORD AS CORRUPTED.

choice1.text: TREAT THE LIVE SIGNAL AS COMPROMISED.

event1.text: Record corrupted. I like that theory.

event3.text: It is emotionally convenient, which makes it suspicious, but I still like it.

event5.text: If the file is wrong, then I am alive and the station is broken.

event12.text: I hate that it waits until I am almost calm.

event1.text: So the signal is the suspicious part.

event6.text: It hurts, but fair and painless rarely share a room.

event8.text: If my signal is compromised, every word I send you could be a door opening from the wrong side.

event10.text: I need you to remember something.

event12.text: If I start sounding too calm, too certain, too helpful... question me.

event6.text: I wish I had not. But I did.

event8.text: A dead engineer with no body. A living signal with my voice. A channel leaving fingerprints.

event10.text: I would like to formally resign from being a mystery.

event3.text: I can do moving. Moving gives panic a job.

event5.text: Emergency carrier control is down the next service spine. I am leaving medical before the signal gets comfortable here.

event3.text: ASK WHO WORE HIS VOICE

event5.text: I heard enough.

event7.text: You listened to it. I understand why. I hate that I understand why.

event9.text: I am moving. Signal is worse now. We brought some of it with us.

event3.text: There was another voice on the emergency channel.

event5.text: Not the unknown one. Not exactly.

event7.text: It sounded close. Like someone behind a wall who already knew my name.

event13.text: Operator... I do not think we are opening a frequency.

event15.text: I think someone is on the other side.

event4.text: No— I said stabilize it first.

event6.text: The channel is open now. We did not lose anyone. But we lost control of how it opens.

event8.text: There is a difference between unlocking a door and having something pull it from the other side.

event2.text: I am not opening it yet. I am watching the pattern first.

event3.text: The ladder shaft is colder than the relay room. Metal was wet under my gloves. Condensation, I hope.

event5.text: Halfway down I heard the carrier pop in my earpiece. Just once. Like someone tapping a microphone.

event7.text: That is why 7.14 worries me. It does not behave like a channel. It behaves like a handle.

event9.text: And someone keeps trying to turn it.

event3.text: The ladder held. My hands did not enjoy the experience, but they have not filed a complaint yet.

event5.text: Personnel cache door is ahead. Half open. The kind of half open that looks less like welcome and more like interruption.

event7.text: I can see dust dragged across the floor near the frame.

event9.text: Someone came through here after the blackout.

event3.text: Door gave way all at once. I hit the frame with my shoulder and pinched the glove seal.

event5.text: It hissed for half a second. Suit patched it. My heart did not.

event9.text: There is a chair on its side by the nearest console. Someone left fast.

event3.text: Bypass strip under the frame. Someone used it recently enough to leave glove residue in the grooves.

event5.text: Not station-recent. Human-recent. There is a difference; I wish there was not.

event7.text: Whoever opened this did not want the door log to notice.

event9.text: I am inside now. Cache terminals are live.

event3.text: Something moved above me halfway through. Heavy. Not debris sliding.

event5.text: I did not look up. That may be the smartest decision I have made all day.

event7.text: A ceiling panel hit the floor behind me. The pressure wave pushed my suit forward.

event9.text: Medical archive door is ahead. My pulse is making editorial comments.

event3.text: There are mugs still magnetized to the wall rack. Lin's is here. Tiny cartoon moon on the side.

event5.text: She hated that mug. Used it every day out of spite because Grant bought it for her.

event10.text: Bad coffee. Cold pizza. Someone complaining the gravity sim made their knees feel old.

event3.text: I passed Door 12 signage on the wall. Old paint. White letters. My stomach reacted before my brain did.

event5.text: I remember the handle. The warning light above it.

event7.text: I remember the seal making a soft click. Ridiculous thing to remember, isn't it?

event9.text: Not the death. Not the person. The click.

event12.text: Someone shouted my name like I had already done something unforgivable.

event14.text: Then a pressure drop. Or the memory of one.

event0.title: VOICE SIGNATURE REPORT
event0.content:
ACTIVE SPEAKER: ETHAN HALE
VOICEPRINT MATCH: 84%
SIGNAL DELAY: NONLINEAR
CARRIER TRACE: 7.14 MHz harmonic residue detected
event0.caption: Recovered voiceprint comparison from live relay traffic.
event0.message: [VOICE SIGNATURE REPORT ARCHIVED TO DATA BANK]

event4.text: High enough to sound like me. Low enough to make every sentence feel borrowed.

event8.text: Even my voice is leaving footprints where it should not be walking.

event1.text: I'm at the carrier control point.

event3.text: The room is smaller than I remembered. Low ceiling. One chair bolted to the floor. Cable bundles like black roots under the console.

event5.text: Channel 7.14 is not open.

event10.text: I do not like systems that wait.

event3.text: I had to stop twice. Not because of the route. Because the signal kept folding my own voice back into my ear.

event5.text: For a second I thought you were talking.

event10.text: The channel is waiting on the console. I can feel it in the speaker before it makes sound.

event3.text: The pattern stabilized when I stopped treating it like noise.

event5.text: There is structure in it. Breath-length gaps. Repeating pressure ticks.

event11.text: Operator... that was not a metaphor.
